summary: O'Neil proposes 'Cathy's Wager,' a philosophy for handling interpersonal friction: always assume others' negative behavior stems from their own struggles rather than anything you did. Inspired by Pascal's Wager, this approach suggests you have nothing to lose by not taking things personally. She illustrates with examples ranging from friends who stop responding to professional no-shows to people who suddenly seem to hate you. The strategy saves time and emotional energy by eliminating futile self-doubt about unchangeable situations. She acknowledges the caveat that this assumes you're not actually an awful person, but notes that awful people already use this logic anyway.
key_points: When someone treats you poorly, default to assuming they're dealing with their own issues rather than responding to something you did
This approach is 'selfish' in a productive way—it preserves your mental energy and prevents endless second-guessing
Not taking things personally actually enables more outreach and relationship repair, not less
The wager has a logical structure: if it's not about you, you've saved yourself grief; if it is about you, you probably can't change it anyway
The only caveat is if you're genuinely awful—but awful people already use this reasoning, so you might as well too
money_quotes: My theory is that you always assume it's something they're going through, and you try to never take it personally.
Err on the side of compassion, not blame.
The assumption that 'it's not about me' is super useful and time-saving.
I don't spend a lot of time second guessing myself or trying to change things that I can't change about other people's feelings.
It's kind of a selfish version of the Serenity Prayer, if you will, without all the religious stuff.
I just don't feel personally affected if it doesn't work. And I think that actually helps me do it more often in the end.
Consider the idea that awful people are already using Cathy's Wager, so you may as well too.
key_insight: Assuming others' negative behavior isn't about you is both emotionally protective and practically effective—a rational bet that costs nothing and preserves your capacity for genuine connection.
